does: This paper introduces ICU-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating continual machine unlearning in multimodal large language models. The benchmark comprises 1,000 privacy-sensitive profiles drawn from two document domains—medical reports and labor contracts—containing 9,500 images, 16,000 question-answer pairs, and 100 sequential forget tasks. Beyond the dataset, the paper proposes novel evaluation metrics that assess forgetting effectiveness, preservation of prior forgetting operations, retained utility on non-target data, and stability across the continual unlearning process. The authors conduct extensive experiments using representative unlearning methods on ICU-Bench to evaluate their performance, revealing limitations in how existing approaches handle sequential privacy deletion requests in realistic deployment scenarios.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on machine unlearning, which emerged as a response to regulatory privacy requirements and the need to remove learned associations from trained models. It extends prior static unlearning benchmarks and methods—which typically consider one-shot deletion requests—to the continual setting where models must handle multiple sequential forget tasks over time. The paper addresses a critical gap identified in recent multimodal learning research, where most benchmarks focus on isolated privacy deletion scenarios rather than the realistic deployment context where privacy requests arrive continuously. By grounding the task in privacy-critical document domains, it connects to real-world applications of document understanding systems and builds on established practices in evaluating machine unlearning through specialized metrics that capture both forgetting quality and knowledge retention.
enables: This benchmark enables future research into more robust continual unlearning methods tailored for multimodal systems, addressing the practical challenge of handling sequential privacy requests without catastrophic forgetting or performance degradation. The introduced metrics provide researchers with standardized tools to measure not just immediate forgetting but also the stability and consistency of prior deletions as new forget tasks arrive, facilitating principled comparison of continual unlearning approaches. The work opens directions for developing techniques that better balance privacy preservation with utility retention across time, relevant for deploying MLLMs in privacy-regulated domains like healthcare and legal services. Additionally, it motivates exploration of efficient continual unlearning that scales to large numbers of forget tasks, potentially leading to architectures and training procedures designed from inception to support incremental privacy deletion without full model retraining.
